title: Prediction and perception: Insights for (and from) tinnitus.

assets.abstract: More than 150 years have passed since Helmholtz first described perception as a process of unconscious inference about the causes of sensations. His ideas have since inspired a wealth of literature investigating the mechanisms underlying these inferences. In recent years, much of this work has converged on the notion that the brain is a hierarchical generative model of its environment that predicts sensations and updates itself based on prediction errors. Here, we build a case for modeling tinni

phase_mapping.bridge_rationale: Dependent arising — predictive processing, free energy principle, active inference as causal cognition.
